title: Signalling Theory and the Credibility Problem in Post-Knowledge Labor Markets

# Signalling Theory and the Credibility Problem in Post-Knowledge Labor Markets

When AI commoditizes knowledge, the economic value of professionals migrates to curiosity, curation, and judgment — capacities that are path-dependent, cognitively expensive, and resistant to replication. The knowledge value chain describes this migration. The notes on curiosity, curation, and judgment describe the properties that make each capacity scarce. What none of these notes addresses is the problem that immediately follows: if the capacities that matter most are precisely the ones that are hardest to observe, how does anyone — an employer, a client, a collaborator, a market — determine who actually possesses them?

This is a signalling problem, and it restructures the upper labor market in ways that the value-chain framework alone cannot predict.

### The collapse of credentialist signalling

Under the old regime, knowledge scarcity made credentials function as reliable signals. A degree from a top institution, a tenure at a prestigious firm, a professional certification — these were costly to acquire (in time, money, and effort), and that costliness is what made them credible. Signalling theory, following Michael Spence, holds that a signal is informative only when it is differentially costly: easy for high-quality candidates to send and prohibitively expensive for low-quality candidates to fake. Credentials met this condition when knowledge was scarce, because acquiring the knowledge that credentials certified was genuinely difficult.

When AI makes knowledge abundant, credentials lose their signalling power — not because they become worthless, but because the underlying asset they certified is no longer scarce. A credential that signals "this person knows corporate finance" conveys less information when an LLM can produce competent corporate finance analysis on demand. The signal has not become false; it has become uninformative. It no longer separates candidates along the dimension that matters, because the dimension that matters has shifted from knowledge possession to curiosity, curation, and judgment — none of which existing credentials were designed to certify.

This creates a signalling vacuum. The capacities that command economic premiums in the restructured value chain are precisely the ones for which no established, institutionally validated signal exists. Judgment is accumulated through path-dependent experience that resists standardized assessment. Curiosity is an orientation that manifests in the quality of inquiry over time, not in a test score. Curation is taste made consequential, and taste is notoriously difficult to credential. The labor market faces a classic information asymmetry: the most valuable workers possess qualities that the most common screening mechanisms cannot detect.

### How curiosity, curation, and judgment are signalled

In the absence of institutional certification, signalling migrates to behavioral and reputational channels — and each of the three scarce capacities develops its own signalling logic.

**Curiosity** is signalled through the quality and trajectory of inquiry. A professional who has pursued a genuinely novel line of investigation — not as a performative gesture but as a sustained commitment with visible consequences — sends a costly signal. Tim Ferriss exploring protein structures, Elon Musk pursuing electric vehicles and reusable rockets when both were considered quixotic, a researcher who spent years on an unfashionable problem before it became central — these are credible signals because they involved real opportunity cost. The person forewent safer, more legible career moves to follow a question. This costliness is what makes the signal informative: someone without genuine curiosity would not bear the cost.

**Curation** is signalled through what one elevates and, critically, what one excludes. A curator's taste becomes legible through a pattern of selections that reveals a coherent but non-obvious sensibility. The venture capitalist who consistently backs companies in categories that do not yet have names, the editor whose publication develops a recognizable intellectual signature, the professional who recommends resources that are genuinely illuminating rather than safely prestigious — each is sending a signal about curatorial judgment that is costly to fake because it requires the underlying capacity to produce. Posting a bestseller list signals nothing; championing an obscure but brilliant work, and being proven right, signals everything.

**Judgment** is signalled through stories of consequential commitment under uncertainty. A track record of decisions where the stakes were real, the outcome was uncertain, and the decision-maker bore the consequences is the only credible signal of judgment. Leaving a secure position for an unproven venture, deploying personal capital in a risky sector, delaying a product launch to address a problem that others would have ignored — these are costly signals because they involve irreversible commitment with genuine downside. The costliness is constitutive: judgment without skin in the game is advice, and advice is cheap.

### The theater problem

Every valuable signal generates an incentive to produce counterfeit versions. This is the theater problem, and it maps directly onto the signalling-theory prediction that low-cost imitations will proliferate whenever a signal becomes associated with high returns.

Curiosity theater takes the form of performative inquiry — asking panel questions that are thinly disguised self-promotion, name-dropping books without engaging their arguments, adopting the vocabulary of exploration without bearing its costs. Curation theater involves assembling selections that mimic taste without embodying it — sharing consensus recommendations, curating for legibility rather than insight, optimizing for the appearance of discernment rather than its substance. Judgment theater manifests as bold pronouncements hedged to meaninglessness, predictions calibrated to be unfalsifiable, and risk-taking narratives that omit the safety nets.

Signalling theory predicts that theater will eventually fail, but the timeline matters. In the short run, theater can capture returns that belong to genuine signal-senders, creating a noise problem that makes the labor market less efficient. In the long run, the costliness condition reasserts itself: sustained curiosity theater is exhausting and produces no compounding returns, because the inquiries lead nowhere. Sustained curation theater is exposed when the curator's selections consistently fail to anticipate what matters. Sustained judgment theater collapses when the hedged predictions are compared against actual outcomes. But the transition period — during which credentialist signals have lost their power and behavioral signals have not yet been widely understood — is one of significant informational friction.

### Status contagion and the luxury-brand dynamic

As behavioral signalling becomes the primary mechanism for communicating curiosity, curation, and judgment, the upper labor market begins to operate through a logic that resembles luxury goods more than commodity markets. In luxury markets, value is sustained not by functional superiority but by the scarcity and credibility of the signal the good sends about its owner. A Hermès bag does not keep belongings safer than a canvas tote; it sends a signal that is costly enough to be credible and exclusive enough to be informative.

Professionals whose curiosity, curation, and judgment have been validated through costly behavioral signals develop a form of reputational capital that functions analogously. Their endorsement transfers credibility — a phenomenon that might be called status contagion. When a respected venture capitalist backs a startup, the startup gains not just capital but the signal of having been selected by someone whose judgment is trusted. When a celebrated editor publishes a new writer, the writer inherits a portion of the editor's curatorial reputation. When a known leader joins a company's board, the company acquires a judgment signal that reduces uncertainty for other stakeholders.

This contagion creates a compounding dynamic. Those with established reputational capital attract more opportunities to exercise their scarce capacities, which generates more signal, which further concentrates trust. The result is a power-law distribution in the upper labor market: a small number of professionals whose signals are widely recognized capture a disproportionate share of economic surplus, while a much larger group — many of whom possess genuine curiosity, curation, and judgment — struggle to make their capacities legible.

### The systemic implication

The signalling problem is not a peripheral concern layered on top of the value-chain restructuring. It is the mechanism through which the restructuring actually manifests in labor markets. The knowledge value chain explains which capacities become scarce. The signalling framework explains how that scarcity is communicated, verified, and rewarded — or, in the case of theater, counterfeited and misallocated.

This has direct implications for organizations. Hiring processes built around credentials and standardized assessments are optimized for detecting the asset that is being commoditized. Detecting curiosity, curation, and judgment requires different instruments: portfolio-based evaluation that examines the trajectory of a candidate's inquiries, reference structures that probe for evidence of consequential commitment, trial engagements that create conditions for genuine judgment to be exercised and observed. Organizations that redesign their talent evaluation around these signals will systematically outperform those that continue to screen for knowledge proxies.

It also has implications for individuals. The strategic priority is not merely to develop curiosity, curation, and judgment — though that is necessary — but to make those capacities legible through signals that are costly enough to be credible. This means taking positions that involve genuine risk, making selections that reveal genuine taste, and pursuing inquiries that involve genuine opportunity cost. The professional who develops excellent judgment but never exercises it in a context where the stakes are visible has an asset that the market cannot price. The signal is not an ornament added to the substance; in a market defined by information asymmetry, the signal is what allows the substance to be economically realized.

The gap between possessing a scarce capacity and having that capacity recognized is the signalling gap, and in a post-knowledge economy, it may be the most consequential gap of all.

## See Also

- [[z20260316-001|The Knowledge Value Chain: How AI Redistributes Economic Value from Knowledge to Judgment]] — grounds the signalling problem in the value-migration framework, showing that the capacities requiring new signals are precisely those to which economic surplus is migrating
- [[z20260316-004|Judgment as the Non-Fungible Residual in an Economy of Abundant Knowledge]] — elaborates the downstream capacity whose signalling is most dependent on costly behavioral evidence, particularly the accountability and trust-compounding properties that make judgment legible only through consequential commitment
- [[z20260316-002|Curiosity as an Economic Asset: The Scarcity of Good Questions in an Age of Cheap Answers]] — describes the upstream capacity whose costliness (cognitive expense, path-dependence, resistance to short-term incentives) is precisely what makes it a credible signal when authentically demonstrated
- [[z20260316-003|Curation: Filtering, Narrative Control, and Power]] — elaborates the intermediate capacity whose signalling operates through patterns of elevation and exclusion, and whose reputational compounding exemplifies the status-contagion dynamic
- [[z20260315-022|AI and the Systemic Restructuring of Work]] — provides the systemic context in which the signalling vacuum emerges, particularly the intrinsic-economic value distinction that explains why possessing a valuable capacity is insufficient without a mechanism to make it legible to markets
